topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The blue light of my phone was the only illumination in my cramped dorm room. I scrolled aimlessly through feeds filled with curated successes—trips, achievements, effortless laughter. Every picture felt like a sharp jab. Then, a notification popped up. It was a direct message from Professor Albright. My breath hitched. My hands, already clammy, started to shake. I clicked it open, and there, a recent photo of me from a group project, was tagged, accompanied by a caption that read, "Great collaboration, [My Name]!" A cold, heavy sensation settled in my chest, squeezing the air from my lungs. I stared at the screen, the words blurring. Why? Was it a mistake? A cruel joke? I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to delete the app, to erase the evidence of this unexpected connection, because being seen, even in this bizarre, public way, felt like a spotlight exposing everything I was trying to hide.

[story 2]

I spent the entire afternoon staring at my reflection in the darkened window. My shoulders slumped, a physical weight pressing down on my neck. The failure of the calculus exam was still a raw, stinging wound. I was just… inadequate. When my phone buzzed, I almost ignored it. It was a message from Professor Albright. It contained a picture of a very mundane campus landmark, captioned with a thoughtful observation about architecture. My fingers hovered over the reply button, a paralyzing hesitation gripping me. It felt like an intrusion, a validation I didn't deserve. But curiosity, that insidious little worm, nudged me forward. I typed a simple, hesitant question about the building. When the reply came back—a brief, polite acknowledgment—a strange flicker of something other than despair crossed my face. It was fleeting, quickly smothered by the familiar tide of self-doubt, leaving me feeling hollowed out and profoundly small.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, amplifying the silence that felt deafening. I was trying to focus on my notes, but my eyes kept drifting to my phone, a nervous tic I couldn't shake. Then, a notification. Professor Albright had liked a post I’d made weeks ago—a poorly rendered piece of digital art I’d posted in a private forum. My stomach clenched. It wasn't praise; it was just an acknowledgement. I physically recoiled, pulling the phone away as if it were burning me. The feeling that I was fundamentally unremarkable, that my efforts amounted to nothing, surged up, making my vision swim slightly. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the sensation down, to make the feeling stop existing. But the image of his profile picture, superimposed over my own shaky hand, kept replaying in my mind, a persistent, unwelcome reminder that I occupied a space, however small, in someone else's digital orbit.
